text: This film depicts our surgical approach to the management of a splenic flexure tumor.
The patient is a thirty six year old female with a four month history of altered bowel habit and an endoscopically confirmed obstructing lesion in the proximal descending colon.
The preoperative CT demonstrated a four point eight centimeter splenic flexure tumor with no evidence of metastatic disease.
The surgeon visually confirms the location of the splenic flexure tumor in the proximal descending colon.
The surgeon applies traction to the inferior mesenteric vein, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a surgical plane between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum, separating these anatomical structures to facilitate further dissection.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein using a ligature, completing the separation of this vessel from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon further develops the plane between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum, enhancing the separation of these tissue layers.
The surgeon develops a plane between the pancreas and the mesocolon at the splenic flexure, carefully separating these anatomical structures.
The surgeon mobilizes the tail of the pancreas from the splenic flexure mesocolon, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon applies traction to the left branch of the middle colic artery, visibly manipulating the vessel within the surgical field.
The surgeon skeletalizes the vessel at its origin, carefully exposing and isolating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon clips and divides the vessel using a Ligasure device, completing the separation of the vessel from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the remaining mesentery, carefully excising all associated lymphatic tissue in an en bloc fashion along the left branch of the middle colic artery.
The surgeon focuses on the descending colon mesentery, preparing to manipulate or dissect this area as part of the procedure.
The surgeon mobilizes the tissue in a medial to lateral direction along the axis of the inferior mesenteric vein, carefully separating the surrounding structures.
The surgeon applies tension to the inferior mesenteric artery pedicle, preparing it for further dissection or division.
The surgeon develops a plane between the inferior mesenteric artery and the urethra peritoneum, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon continues mobilization of the descending colon mesentery in a medial to lateral fashion, following the axis of the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon applies tension to the trifurcation area involving the inferior mesenteric artery, superior hemorrhoidal artery, and left colic artery, highlighting these vascular structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon skeletalizes the left colic artery and vein at their origin, carefully exposing these vessels from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon clips and divides the vessels individually using a ligature, focusing on the previously exposed vascular structures.
The surgeon completes the medial mobilization of the colon, finalizing the separation of tissue planes in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the lateral mobilization of the colon, following the completion of medial mobilization and vessel division.
The ureter is clearly visible in the surgical field, identifiable as a tubular structure running parallel to the colon.
The surgeon continues the lateral mobilization of the colon, advancing towards the spleen, with visible separation of tissue planes.
The surgeon separates the omentum from the transverse colon, carefully detaching the connective tissue between these structures.
The surgeon reveals the medial plane of dissection along the tail of the pancreas, showing clear separation from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon completes the immobilization of the splenic flexure by carefully dissecting the mesentery and associated omentum from the tail of the pancreas, ensuring clear separation of these structures.
The tail of the pancreas is fully exposed, with surrounding tissues and structures clearly visible following dissection.
The surgical specimen is extracted through the umbilical incision, resected, and an end-to-end anastomosis is manually constructed.
The surgeon confirms perfusion, likely assessing the blood flow to the newly anastomosed section of the colon.
Final pathology revealed a T-III N-I tumour with one of forty-one lymph nodes positive.
The patient was discharged well to home on post-operative day four.
Thank you.
